items.subtopic: By Size of Red Cell | HYPERPRODUCTION: HEMOLYSIS/BLEEDING
items.title: Elevated Reticulocyte Count and Hemolysis Indicators
items.content: An elevated reticulocyte count suggests hemolysis, characterized by accelerated red cell breakdown. This can be due to intrinsic or extrinsic causes, with laboratory findings including high LDH, low haptoglobin, increased free hemoglobin, and specific blood smear abnormalities like schistocytes or spherocytes.
keyPoints: Elevated reticulocyte count indicates hemolysis
Laboratory markers: High LDH, Low haptoglobin, Increased free hemoglobin
Blood smear may show schistocytes or spherocytes
Autoimmune hemolytic anemia presents with spherocytes, high LDH, and low haptoglobin
items.reference: By Size of Red Cell · By Size of Red Cell | HYPERPRODUCTION: HEMOLYSIS/BLEEDING | UNDERPRODUCTION | NUTRITIONAL

items.subtopic: By Size of Red Cell | UNDERPRODUCTION
items.title: Common Causes of Hypoproliferative Anemia
items.content: Most cases of anemia (75%) are hypoproliferative due to impaired red cell production. Common causes include iron deficiency, inflammation, and lack of erythropoietin (EPO) from conditions like renal disease or aging.
keyPoints: 75% of anemias are hypoproliferative
Common causes: Iron deficiency, Inflammation, Lack of EPO
items.reference: By Size of Red Cell · By Size of Red Cell | HYPERPRODUCTION: HEMOLYSIS/BLEEDING | UNDERPRODUCTION | NUTRITIONAL

items.subtopic: By Size of Red Cell | NUTRITIONAL
items.title: Iron and Vitamin B12 Deficiency in Anemia
items.content: Iron is crucial for hemoglobin synthesis, with deficiencies leading to microcytic anemia. Serum ferritin <30 µg/L indicates depleted iron stores. Vitamin B12 deficiency impairs DNA synthesis, causing macrocytosis and hypersegmented neutrophils.
keyPoints: Iron: 1 billion atoms per red cell; serum ferritin <30 µg/L indicates depletion
Vitamin B12 deficiency leads to macrocytosis and hypersegmented neutrophils
items.reference: By Size of Red Cell · By Size of Red Cell | HYPERPRODUCTION: HEMOLYSIS/BLEEDING | UNDERPRODUCTION | NUTRITIONAL

items.subtopic: MARROW REPLACEMENT | LACK OF ERYTHROPOIETIN | ABSENCE OF RED CELL PRECURSORS
items.title: Marrow Replacement and Its Impact on Anemia
items.content: Infections such as histoplasmosis or tuberculosis, along with neoplasms like metastatic prostate cancer or multiple myeloma, can invade the bone marrow, leading to anemia by crowding out hematopoietic elements. A blood smear in these cases may show a myelophthisic picture characterized by nucleated red cells, teardrop cells, and immature white cells, necessitating a marrow examination.
keyPoints: Infections (e.g., histoplasmosis, tuberculosis) and neoplasms can lead to marrow replacement.
Marrow replacement can cause anemia, with severe cases seen in metastatic prostate cancer or multiple myeloma.
Blood smear findings include nucleated red cells, teardrop cells, and immature white cells, indicating a need for marrow examination.
items.reference: By Size of Red Cell · MARROW REPLACEMENT | LACK OF ERYTHROPOIETIN | ABSENCE OF RED CELL PRECURSORS

items.subtopic: MARROW REPLACEMENT | LACK OF ERYTHROPOIETIN | ABSENCE OF RED CELL PRECURSORS
items.title: Erythropoietin Deficiency and Anemia of Inflammation
items.content: Anemia can result from a lack of erythropoietin (EPO), primarily due to renal disease, where anemia is almost always present with a glomerular filtration rate of <30 mL/min/1.73 m². Inflammatory cytokines like tumor necrosis factor decrease EPO production and increase hepcidin levels, blocking iron absorption and release from stores. Anemia is prevalent in older adults, affecting 11% of community-dwelling and up to 40% of nursing home residents over age 65.
keyPoints: Renal disease with a glomerular filtration rate <30 mL/min/1.73 m² often leads to anemia due to EPO deficiency.
Inflammatory cytokines decrease EPO production and increase hepcidin, reducing iron availability.
Anemia prevalence is 11% in community-dwelling and up to 40% in nursing home residents over age 65.
items.reference: By Size of Red Cell · MARROW REPLACEMENT | LACK OF ERYTHROPOIETIN | ABSENCE OF RED CELL PRECURSORS

items.subtopic: STEM CELL DEFECTS | EVALUATION OF ANEMIA
items.title: Recognizing Myelodysplastic Syndromes and Leukemia in Anemia Evaluation
items.content: Myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS) and leukemias can present with anemia, often requiring bone marrow testing for diagnosis. MDS may manifest as macrocytic anemia or pancytopenia, while acute myelogenous leukemia (AML) and chronic myelogenous leukemia (CML) frequently show severe anemia alongside elevated white cell counts.
keyPoints: MDS and leukemias can present with anemia; bone marrow testing is crucial for diagnosis.
Patients with MDS may have macrocytic anemia or pancytopenia.
AML and CML often show severe anemia with elevated white cell counts.
items.reference: By Size of Red Cell · STEM CELL DEFECTS | EVALUATION OF ANEMIA

items.subtopic: STEM CELL DEFECTS | EVALUATION OF ANEMIA
items.title: Guiding Anemia Workup by Reticulocyte Count and MCV
items.content: The reticulocyte count helps determine if anemia is due to decreased production or increased destruction. A high reticulocyte count suggests hemolysis, prompting tests like LDH, haptoglobin, and direct antibody testing. If the reticulocyte count is low, the mean corpuscular volume (MCV) guides further workup: a low MCV indicates potential iron deficiency, while macrocytosis may suggest megaloblastic anemia or myelodysplasia.
keyPoints: High reticulocyte count suggests hemolysis; test with LDH, haptoglobin, and direct antibody testing.
Low reticulocyte count: low MCV indicates iron deficiency; assess ferritin.
Macrocytosis: check for megaloblastic anemia (hypersegmented neutrophils) or myelodysplasia.
items.reference: By Size of Red Cell · STEM CELL DEFECTS | EVALUATION OF ANEMIA

items.subtopic: Anemia | POLYCYTHEMIA
items.title: Indications for Blood Transfusion in Anemia
items.content: Blood transfusions are indicated in anemic patients presenting with hypotension or signs of cardiac compromise such as angina. This is particularly relevant during the diagnostic workup and for those with bone marrow failure awaiting definitive therapy.
keyPoints: Indications for blood transfusion include hypotension or cardiac compromise (e.g., angina).
Transfusions may be necessary to support patients during diagnostic workup or while awaiting definitive therapy, especially in cases of bone marrow failure.
items.reference: By Size of Red Cell · Anemia | POLYCYTHEMIA

items.subtopic: Anemia | POLYCYTHEMIA
items.title: Expanded Therapeutic Options for Anemias
items.content: Over the past 30 years, therapeutic options for anemias have expanded significantly. These include blood component therapy, recombinant EPO for chronic renal failure and chemotherapy-induced anemia, and other targeted therapies such as TGF-β inhibitors for myelodysplastic syndrome.
keyPoints: Blood component therapy is available and safe.
Recombinant EPO has transformed management in chronic renal failure and reduced transfusion needs in anemic cancer patients.
TGF-β inhibitors are used for anemia associated with myelodysplastic syndrome.
items.reference: By Size of Red Cell · Anemia | POLYCYTHEMIA

items.subtopic: Anemia | POLYCYTHEMIA
items.title: Diagnostic Thresholds and Clinical Features of Polycythemia
items.content: Polycythemia is characterized by elevated hemoglobin levels, with concern typically raised at 17 g/dL for men and 15 g/dL for women. Hematocrit levels >50% in men or >45% in women may be abnormal, while levels >60% in men and >55% in women almost invariably indicate increased red cell mass.
keyPoints: Concern for polycythemia is triggered at hemoglobin levels of 17 g/dL (men) and 15 g/dL (women).
Hematocrit levels >50% (men) or >45% (women) may be abnormal; levels >60% (men) or >55% (women) almost invariably indicate increased red cell mass.
Symptoms of polycythemia include hyperviscosity-related issues such as neurologic symptoms and hypertension.
items.reference: By Size of Red Cell · Anemia | POLYCYTHEMIA

items.subtopic: Polycythemia
items.title: Distinguishing Polycythemia Vera from Secondary Causes
items.content: To differentiate polycythemia vera from secondary causes, assess red cell mass and serum EPO levels. If the red cell mass is increased (>36 mL/kg in men, >32 mL/kg in women) with low or unmeasurable EPO levels, consider polycythemia vera. Confirm with JAK2 mutation testing.
keyPoints: Increased red cell mass: >36 mL/kg in men, >32 mL/kg in women
Low/unmeasurable serum EPO suggests polycythemia vera
JAK2 (Val617Phe) mutation found in 90–95% of patients with polycythemia vera
Check for JAK2 mutations and perform abdominal ultrasound if EPO is low
items.reference: By Size of Red Cell · Polycythemia

items.subtopic: Polycythemia
items.title: Evaluating Elevated Serum EPO Levels
items.content: When serum EPO levels are elevated, determine if it's due to hypoxia or autonomous production. Evaluate O₂ saturation: <92% suggests heart/lung disease; normal saturation with smoking indicates 'smoker’s polycythemia'. High COHb confirms smoker’s polycythemia.
keyPoints: Elevated EPO with O₂ saturation >93% suggests secondary causes
<92% O₂ saturation warrants evaluation for heart/lung disease
Normal O₂ saturation in smokers may indicate 'smoker’s polycythemia'
High COHb levels confirm smoker’s polycythemia
items.reference: By Size of Red Cell · Polycythemia

items.question: What is the primary difference between erythrocytosis and polycythemia?
options: Erythrocytosis implies increased red cell mass, while polycythemia refers to any increase in red cells.
Polycythemia implies increased red cell mass, while erythrocytosis refers to any increase in red cells.
Both terms are used interchangeably with no difference.
Erythrocytosis is associated with hypoxemia, whereas polycythemia is not.
items.explanation: Erythrocytosis implies increased red cell mass, while polycythemia refers to any increase in red cells.

items.assertion: Iron deficiency is the most common nutritional deficiency worldwide.
items.reason: This is because iron is required for hemoglobin synthesis, and its deficiency leads to microcytic anemia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why iron deficiency is prevalent.

items.reason: In countries with folate-fortified flour, deficiencies are rare unless there is severe malabsorption or a very poor diet.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not fully explain the elimination of deficiencies as they can still occur under certain conditions.
